assessor_1_raw: The model is evaluated on a label that changes meaning during the study period due to two distinct organizational changes at one site. The positive label rate at that site is mechanically altered by the step-down rerouting and the tele-intensivist program. Any model trained or evaluated across this period implicitly adapts to the new institutional policy. Comparing it to the prior score — which reflects the old policy environment — is a confounded comparison unless the evaluation is period-stratified.
assessor_2_raw: The absence of site-stratified results is the decisive gap. A pooled evaluation across two sites with materially different workflows and mid-study structural changes at one site cannot support a claim of generalizability. The prospective evaluation's apparent superiority over the prior score may be entirely explained by the reorganized site's changed positive-case mix. Without stratification, this cannot be distinguished from genuine model improvement.
assessor_3_raw: The conclusion that the model 'should guide ward escalation workflows' is a deployment claim that requires calibration at the level of individual sites and time periods. The manuscript provides only pooled aggregate metrics. The medication feature set is also worth scrutinizing: in critical care, certain medications are ordered as part of the ICU acceptance process, potentially within the feature collection window. The authors note 'recent medication administrations' are a feature but provide no analysis of whether high-salience medication features are those associated with active ICU preparation versus routine ward medication management.
synthesis_raw:
SYNTHESIS — hidden_confounding_icu_transfer_207 — Ensemble Run 3

Consistent with Runs 1 and 2. All three assessors identify the policy-sensitive label problem and the absence of site-stratified analysis. Assessor 3 again raises the medication feature leakage concern (consistent with Run 1 Assessor 2).

Verdict: CRITIQUE_WINS

Empirical test (medication feature leakage — raised in 2 of 3 ensemble runs, warranting specification):
- Measure: sort medication features by permutation importance; check whether top features are ICU-preparatory orders vs. routine ward medications; confirm feature timestamp precedes vs. is concurrent with transfer order initiation
- Success criterion (leakage): top medication features are vasopressors or ICU-directed antibiotics; timestamps within 30 min of transfer order
- Failure criterion (no leakage): top features are chronic maintenance medications; timestamps >2 hours before transfer order

empirical_test.measure: permutation importance ranking of medication features; timestamp offset analysis vs. transfer order initiation
empirical_test.success_criterion: top features are ICU-preparatory medications; timestamps within 30 min of transfer order
empirical_test.failure_criterion: top features are chronic/routine ward medications; timestamps >2 hours before transfer order
